This adds a `terminal_features` config option that lets users declare which terminal programs support progress notifications.

## Changes

- **`src/terminal/features.go`** — new `TerminalFeatures` struct with a `Progress []string` field; gob-registered for config cache compatibility
- **`src/terminal/writer.go`** — replaced hardcoded `Program != WindowsTerminal` check with `slices.Contains(progressTerminals, Program)`; `Init()` seeds the default (`["Windows Terminal"]`); new `SetProgressTerminals()` setter
- **`src/config/config.go`** — added `TerminalFeatures *terminal.TerminalFeatures` field (`terminal_features` in serialized config)
- **`src/cli/upgrade.go`** — reordered startup: config is now loaded before `StartProgress()` so terminal features take effect
- **`themes/schema.json`** — added `terminal_features.progress` array property

## Example config

```yaml
terminal_features:
  progress:
    - ghostty
    - Windows Terminal
```

Backward compatible: if `terminal_features` is not set, Windows Terminal remains the only supported terminal (existing behavior unchanged).

- **Never use `else`** — use early returns, `continue`, or `break` instead; keep the happy path left-aligned

- Interfaces use `-er` suffix; keep them small (1–3 methods); accept interfaces, return concrete types
- Wrap errors with `fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err)`; name error vars `err`; error strings start lowercase
- Log with the codebase `log` package only; use `defer log.Trace(time.Now(), args)` for complex calls
- Line length max 180 characters
- **Pre-commit gate** (all must pass before committing):
  `modernize --fix ./...`, `fieldalignment --fix ./...`, `go mod tidy`, `gofmt -w .`, `golangci-lint run`
- Always use named fields in struct literals so `fieldalignment` rewrites don't break positional init
- When adding platform-specific files (`_unix.go`, `_windows.go`), cross-compile for the other platform

1	package cli
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"strings"
6	
7		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/cache"
8		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/cli/font"
9		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/dsc"
10		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/log"
11		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/runtime"
12		"github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/src/terminal"
13	
14		"github.com/spf13/cobra"
15	)
16	
17	var (
18		zipFolder string
19		headless  bool
20	
21		fontCmd = &cobra.Command{
22			Use:   "font [install|configure]",
23			Short: "Manage fonts",
24			Long: `Manage fonts.
25	
26	This command is used to install fonts and configure the font in your terminal.
27	
28	  - install: oh-my-posh font install 3270`,
29			ValidArgs: []string{
30				"install",
31				"configure",
32			},
33			Run: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
34				if len(args) == 0 {
35					_ = cmd.Help()
36					return
37				}
38				switch args[0] {
39				case "install":
40					var fontName string
41					if len(args) > 1 {
42						fontName = args[1]
43					}

Support progress for more terminals
---
### Code of Conduct

- [x] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

### What would you like to see added?

While working on #7377 I noticed that OSC 9 progress sequences are only supported on Windows Terminal, but other terminals like Ghostty supports these. Instead of hardcoding specific program support, how about letting users map supported features to supported terminals? E.g., if there was a feature named "progress" people could map `$TERM_PROGRAM` to supported features e.g.,

```yaml
shell_features:
  progress:
  - Ghostty
  - Windows Terminal
```

content.text:
Review complete. Here's my analysis of [PR #7542](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/pull/7542).

## Overview

The PR (authored by the Copilot agent) implements [#7379](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7379): OSC 9;4 progress sequences were gated on `Program == WindowsTerminal`, and this makes the allowlist configurable via a new `terminal_features.progress` config option. The approach is sound and matches what the issue asked for — a user-declared mapping instead of hardcoded terminal support, with the Windows Terminal default preserved for backward compatibility. Tests pass locally. But there are two things that will break CI and a few design gaps worth fixing before merge.

## Blocking issues

- **`gofmt` fails on [config.go:73](src/config/config.go:73).** The struct tag columns weren't re-aligned when the `TerminalFeatures` field was inserted — `gofmt -l` flags the file (I verified on this checkout). The repo's pre-commit gate (`gofmt -w`, `fieldalignment --fix`, `golangci-lint`) was clearly not run.
- **Name stutter: `terminal.TerminalFeatures`.** The project's Go conventions explicitly ban stuttering. This should be `terminal.Features` (the existing `terminal.ITermFeatures` reads fine because the prefix differs).

## Design concerns

- **Case-sensitive matching will bite users.** `slices.Contains` does an exact match against `$TERM_PROGRAM`. Ghostty reports `ghostty` (lowercase), yet the issue's own example config says `Ghostty` — that config would silently do nothing. Matching with `strings.EqualFold` would make this forgiving at negligible cost, since these functions run at most a handful of times per command.
- **The `font install` command doesn't get the feature.** [tui.go:296](src/cli/font/tui.go:296) also emits `StartProgress`/`StopProgress`, but [font.go](src/cli/font.go) calls `terminal.Init` without ever loading config, so a Ghostty user gets progress during upgrade but not during font install. Either wire it up there too, or centralize the wiring (e.g., have config application push the list into the terminal package once) so future callers can't miss it.
- **An explicit empty list can't disable progress.** The guard in [upgrade.go:78](src/cli/upgrade.go:78) (`!= nil && len(...) > 0`) means `progress: []` is treated the same as "not configured" and silently keeps the Windows Terminal default. If someone wants to turn progress off entirely, they can't. Dropping the `len` check (nil-check only) would give the empty list its natural meaning.
- **Pointer field is avoidable.** `*terminal.TerminalFeatures` exists only to enable the nil check above. A value field with a `len(cfg.TerminalFeatures.Progress)` check would be simpler and consistent with how `ITermFeatures` is declared — though if you adopt "empty list disables", the pointer distinction becomes meaningful, so decide these two together. The `gob.Register` in features.go is harmless but likely unnecessary — gob only needs registration for interface-typed values, and this is a concrete struct field (the `ITermFeatures` registration it mimics exists for the same questionable reason).

## Smaller points

- **Documentation is missing.** `iterm_features` and every other general setting is documented in [general.mdx:140](website/docs/configuration/general.mdx:140); `terminal_features` isn't mentioned anywhere on the website. The schema.json entry is good, but the docs table needs a row (and ideally a note that values match `$TERM_PROGRAM`).
- **Tests leak global state.** `TestProgressFunctions` mutates package globals `Program` and `progressTerminals` and leaves them at `"alacritty"` / `["ghostty"]` after the last case. Tests in other files in this package that run afterward would see that. A `t.Cleanup` restoring both would make this safe. Also, the reset value `[]string{WindowsTerminal}` duplicates the default in `Init` rather than deriving from it.
- **Upgrade flow reorder is correct but slightly visible:** config now loads (which can involve fetching a remote config) before the progress indicator starts, so there's a brief window with no progress state where there used to be one. Not worth blocking on.

## Verdict

Right shape, wrong finish. The core mechanism (configurable allowlist, default seeded in `Init`, setter applied after config load) is the correct minimal design, and test coverage of the new behavior is decent. But it needs: the pre-commit gate run (gofmt failure is a hard CI blocker), the rename to `terminal.Features`, case-insensitive matching, website docs, and a decision on the font-install gap and empty-list semantics. I'd request changes.

# Go

Refer to `skills/golang/SKILL.md` for the full Go coding standards.

Key rules to always apply:

- **Never use `else`** — use early returns, `continue`, or `break` instead; keep the happy path left-aligned
- Names use `mixedCaps`/`MixedCaps`; avoid `_` in package names; no stuttering (`http.Server` not `http.HTTPServer`)
- Interfaces use `-er` suffix; keep them small (1–3 methods); accept interfaces, return concrete types
- Wrap errors with `fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err)`; name error vars `err`; error strings start lowercase
- Log with the codebase `log` package only; use `defer log.Trace(time.Now(), args)` for complex calls
- Line length max 180 characters
- **Pre-commit gate** (all must pass before committing):
  `modernize --fix ./...`, `fieldalignment --fix ./...`, `go mod tidy`, `gofmt -w .`, `golangci-lint run`
- Always use named fields in struct literals so `fieldalignment` rewrites don't break positional init
- When adding platform-specific files (`_unix.go`, `_windows.go`), cross-compile for the other platform

Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\conventional-commit

# Conventional Commit

## Commit Message Structure

```text
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type       | When to use                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `feat`     | A new feature                                          |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                              |
| `docs`     | Documentation changes, no code                         |
| `style`    | Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that is neither a fix nor a feature        |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                                |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration changes                               |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks (updating deps, tooling, etc.)       |
| `revert`   | Reverts a previous commit                              |

Append `!` after the type/scope to signal a **breaking change**: `feat!:` or `feat(api)!:`
When a change breaks existing behavior, **both markers are mandatory**: the `!` suffix on the type
**and** the `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. They always appear together — never one without the other.

### Scope

Optional. Use the name of the area affected, e.g., `segment`, `cache`, `config`, `ui`.
Omit when the change is truly cross-cutting.

### Description

- Required. One short imperative sentence, no period at the end. The full header line (type +
  scope + description) must be **72 characters or fewer**. Aim for **50 characters or fewer for
  the description itself** — this almost always keeps the full header within budget regardless
  of type and scope length.
- Use the imperative mood: "add", not "added" or "adds". Never past tense or present-third-person:
  ✗ `added`, `fixed`, `bumped`, `implemented` → ✓ `add`, `fix`, `bump`, `implement`.
- **Never mirror the input's phrasing.** If the request uses past-tense words (`updated`, `added`,
  `bumped`, `was removed`, `got regenerated`), convert them to imperative before writing the
  description: `update`, `add`, `bump`, `remove`, `regenerate`.

### Body

Optional. Add context about _why_ the change was made, not _what_. The diff shows that.
Wrap at 72 characters.

### Footer

Use for:

- `BREAKING CHANGE: <description>` (required when `!` is used; explains the break).
- Issue references: `Closes #123`, `Fixes #456`.
- Co-authors: `Co-Authored-By: Name <email>`.

## Workflow

1. Run `git status` to review changed files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
3. Identify the **type** from the table above. Ask yourself: does this change **remove, rename, or
   alter existing behavior** that callers depend on? If yes → it is a breaking change: use `!`
   after the type/scope **and** add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. Both markers are always required
   together.
4. Identify the **scope** from the files/area changed.
5. Write a short **description** in the imperative mood.
6. Add a **body** if the _why_ needs explanation.
7. Add a **footer** for breaking changes or issue references.
8. Stage the relevant files explicitly (avoid `git add -A`).
9. Commit with a message that preserves multi-line formatting when body/footer are present.

## Examples

```text
feat(segment): add Ramadan segment with Aladhan API
fix(cache): always store mod time
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(config): simplify option parsing logic
chore(deps): bump github.com/shirou/gopsutil/v4
feat(segment)!: rename template property StartTime to Start

BREAKING CHANGE: template strings using .StartTime must be updated to .Start
```

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Type is one of the allowed values in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] The commit message respects the rules defined in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] Scope (if present) reflects the actual area changed
- [ ] Description is imperative mood, no trailing period
- [ ] Full header line (type + scope + description) is 72 characters or fewer
- [ ] Both `!` after type/scope **and** `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer are present whenever the change breaks existing behavior
- [ ] No sensitive files staged (.env, credentials, etc.)

input.command:
gh pr edit 7542 --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
OSC 9;4 progress sequences were hardcoded to Windows Terminal, but other terminals (e.g. Ghostty) also support them. This adds a `terminal_features` config option that lets users declare which terminal programs support progress notifications.

## Changes

- **`src/terminal/features.go`** — new `terminal.Features` struct with a `Progress []string` field and a nil-safe `Apply()` method that activates the configured features
- **`src/terminal/writer.go`** — progress functions check the configured list, matched case-insensitively against the terminal program; `Init()` seeds the default (`Windows Terminal`)
- **`src/config/config.go`** — new `terminal_features` config field
- **`src/cli/upgrade.go`** / **`src/cli/font.go`** — load the config and apply terminal features before emitting progress sequences (`font install` shows progress too)
- **`themes/schema.json`** — schema for `terminal_features.progress`
- **`website/docs/configuration/general.mdx`** — settings table entry and a *Terminal features* section

## Example config

```yaml
terminal_features:
  progress:
    - ghostty
    - Windows Terminal
```

## Behavior

- not set: Windows Terminal only (existing behavior unchanged)
- empty list: progress sequences disabled
- values are matched case-insensitively against `$TERM_PROGRAM` (Windows Terminal is detected via `WT_SESSION`)

Closes #7379

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"

content.text:
All review findings are fixed, amended into the feature commit (`bf395534`), and force-pushed to [PR #7542](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/pull/7542). The PR description is updated to match.

What changed:

- **Rename**: `terminal.TerminalFeatures` → `terminal.Features`, removing the name stutter.
- **Case-insensitive matching**: the progress check now uses `strings.EqualFold` against `$TERM_PROGRAM` via a shared `progressSupported()` helper, so `Ghostty` and `ghostty` both work.
- **Empty-list semantics**: `SetProgressTerminals` is replaced by a nil-safe `Features.Apply()` method — `terminal_features` absent keeps the Windows Terminal default, while an explicit empty list now disables progress sequences entirely.
- **Font install gap**: `oh-my-posh font install` now loads the config and applies terminal features, so configured terminals get progress there too, not just during upgrade.
- **Formatting**: `gofmt` fixed the struct tag alignment in config.go that would have failed CI.
- **Docs**: added a `terminal_features` row to the settings table and a *Terminal features* section in [general.mdx](website/docs/configuration/general.mdx) explaining the matching rules; the [schema.json](themes/schema.json) description now points at those docs and spells out the default and empty-list behavior.
- **Tests**: rewrote `TestProgressFunctions` to use `Features.Apply()`, restore the package globals via `t.Cleanup`, and cover two new cases (case-insensitive match, empty list disables). All eight cases pass.
- **Commit message**: the amended commit now follows conventional commits (`feat(terminal): make progress sequence terminals configurable`) with a `Closes #7379` footer, preserving the original author and co-author trailers.

Verification: `modernize`, `fieldalignment`, `go mod tidy`, `gofmt`, and `golangci-lint` all pass; tests for `terminal`, `config`, and `cli/...` are green; markdownlint on the docs page is back to the file's pre-existing baseline (my edits add zero new findings); schema.json parses.

One thing to be aware of: the remote PR branch was still the original Copilot version based on an older main, while your local checkout had already been rebased onto current main — the force-push (with lease) updated the PR to the rebased branch plus the fixes, so the PR now also reflects the rebase. CI checks are running on the new head.
